---
title: "ST5209/X Assignment 2"
format: pdf
editor: visual
author: "Sivakumar Nishaanth"
---

## Submission

1.  Render the document to get a .pdf printout.
2.  Submit both the .qmd and .pdf files to Canvas.

```{r}
#| message: FALSE
#| echo: FALSE
library(tidyverse)
library(fpp3)
```

## Question 1 (Forecast evaluation, Q5.12 in FPP)

`tourism` contains quarterly visitor nights (in thousands) from 1998 to 2017 for 76 regions of Australia.

```{r}
tourism
```

a.  Extract data from the Gold Coast region using `filter()` and aggregate total overnight trips using `summarise()`. Call this new dataset `gc_tourism`.

    ```{r}
    gc_tourism <- tourism |>
      filter(Region == "Gold Coast") |>
      summarise(Total_Trips = sum(Trips))

    gc_tourism
    ```

b.  Using `slice()` or `filter()`, create three training sets for this data excluding the last 1, 2 and 3 years. For example, `gc_train_1 <- gc_tourism |> slice(1:(n()-4))`.

    ```{r}
    gc_train_1 <- gc_tourism |>
      slice(1:(n() - 4))

    gc_train_2 <- gc_tourism |>
      slice(1:(n() - 8))

    gc_train_3 <- gc_tourism |>
      slice(1:(n() - 12))
    ```

c.  Compute and plot one year of forecasts for each training set using the seasonal naive (`SNAIVE()`) method. Call these `gc_fc_1`, `gc_fc_2` and `gc_fc_3`, respectively. (Hint: You may combine the mable objects from each model into a single mable using `bind_cols`)

    ```{r}
    gc_fc_1 <- gc_train_1 |>
      model(SNAIVE = SNAIVE(Total_Trips)) |>
      forecast(h = 4)

    autoplot(gc_train_1, Total_Trips) +
      autolayer(gc_fc_1, level = NULL) +
      labs(title = "Gold Coast: SNAIVE forecast (train excludes last 1 year)")
    ```

    ```{r}
    gc_fc_2 <- gc_train_2 |>
      model(SNAIVE = SNAIVE(Total_Trips)) |>
      forecast(h = 4)

    autoplot(gc_train_2, Total_Trips) +
      autolayer(gc_fc_2, level = NULL) +
      labs(title = "Gold Coast: SNAIVE forecast (train excludes last 2 years)")
    ```

    ```{r}
    gc_fc_3 <- gc_train_3 |>
      model(SNAIVE = SNAIVE(Total_Trips)) |>
      forecast(h = 4)

    autoplot(gc_train_3, Total_Trips) +
      autolayer(gc_fc_3, level = NULL) +
      labs(title = "Gold Coast: SNAIVE forecast (train excludes last 3 years)")
    ```

    ```{r}
    fit_1 <- gc_train_1 |> model(SNAIVE_1 = SNAIVE(Total_Trips))
    fit_2 <- gc_train_2 |> model(SNAIVE_2 = SNAIVE(Total_Trips))
    fit_3 <- gc_train_3 |> model(SNAIVE_3 = SNAIVE(Total_Trips))
    fits_all <- bind_cols(fit_1, fit_2, fit_3)
    gc_fc_all <- fits_all |> forecast(h = 4)
    autoplot(gc_tourism, Total_Trips) +
      autolayer(gc_fc_all, level = NULL) +
      labs(title = "Gold Coast: SNAIVE 1-Year Forecasts")
    ```

d.  Use `accuracy()` to compare the test set forecast accuracy using MASE. What can you conclude about the relative performance of the different models? Explain your answer.

    ```{r}
    acc_1 <- accuracy(gc_fc_1, gc_tourism)
    acc_2 <- accuracy(gc_fc_2, gc_tourism)
    acc_3 <- accuracy(gc_fc_3, gc_tourism)

    accuracy_comparison <- bind_rows(
      acc_1 |> mutate(model = "Gold Coast: SNAIVE forecast (train excludes last 1 years)"),
      acc_2 |> mutate(model = "Gold Coast: SNAIVE forecast (train excludes last 2 years)"),
      acc_3 |> mutate(model = "Gold Coast: SNAIVE forecast (train excludes last 3 years)")
    ) |>
      select(model, MASE) |>
      arrange(MASE)

    print(accuracy_comparison)
    ```

    *Using the MASE values from the test set accuracy comparison, the model trained by excluding the last two years performs best, with the lowest MASE (MASE = 0.67). The model excluding the last three years performs moderately (MASE = 1.46), while the model excluding only the last one year performs worst (MASE = 2.66).*

    *Since a lower MASE indicates better forecast accuracy, this suggests that the training window excluding the last two years provides the most reliable seasonal naive forecasts for the test period. The model excluding only one year performs poorly, possibly because the most recent observations differ structurally from earlier patterns, reducing the effectiveness of the seasonal naive assumption.*

## Question 2 (ACF plots, Q2.9 in FPP)

The following time plots and ACF plots correspond to four different time series. Match each time plot in the first row with one of the ACF plots in the second row.

![](../_images/acfguess-1.png)

1.  Daily temperature of cow → B
2.  Monthly accidental deaths → A
3.  Monthly air passengers → D
4.  Annual mink trappings → C

## Question 3 (Time series classification)

We will investigate this [dataset](https://kdd.ics.uci.edu/databases/synthetic_control/synthetic_control.html), which contains 600 synthetic control charts for an industrial process. There are 6 types of control charts, and our goal is to build a model to classify them correctly.

We have already processed the raw data into a convenient, labeled form. Run the following code snippet to load it and create train and test sets.

```{r}
ccharts <- read_rds("../_data/CLEANED/ccharts.rds")
ccharts_train <- ccharts[["train"]]
ccharts_test <- ccharts[["test"]]
```

a.  Make a time plot of one time series from each category in the training set. Note that the category is recorded under the `Type` column.

    ```{r}
    levels(ccharts_train$Type)
    ```

    ```{r}
    one_id_each <- ccharts_train |>
      group_by(Type) |>
      summarise(id = first(id), .groups = "drop")

    one_series_each <- ccharts_train |>
      semi_join(one_id_each, by = c("Type", "id"))

    ggplot(one_series_each, aes(x = Time, y = value, colour = Type)) +
      geom_line() +
      facet_wrap(~ Type, scales = "free_y") +
      labs(title = "One Time Series from Each Control Chart Category")
    ```

b.  Compute all time series features for both the training and test set using the snippet. What is the difference between `acf1` and `stl_e_acf1` for Increasing, Decreasing, Upward, and Downward types? Why is there a difference?

```{r}
#| eval: TRUE
# Change to eval: TRUE in order to run
train_feats <- ccharts_train |> features(value, feature_set(pkgs = "feasts"))
test_feats <- ccharts_test |> features(value, feature_set(pkgs = "feasts"))
```

```{r}
train_feats |>
filter(Type %in% c("Increasing", "Decreasing", "Upward", "Downward")) |>
select(Type, acf1, stl_e_acf1)
```

```{r}
train_feats |>
  filter(Type %in% c("Increasing", "Decreasing", "Upward", "Downward")) |>
  group_by(Type) |>
  summarise(
    mean_acf1 = mean(acf1),
    mean_stl_e_acf1 = mean(stl_e_acf1)
  )
```

*For Increasing, Decreasing, Upward, and Downward types, the lag-1 autocorrelation (acf1) is high (around 0.7) because the presence of trend or level shifts induces strong serial dependence between consecutive observations.*

*However, after removing the trend using STL decomposition, the autocorrelation of the remainder (stl_e_acf1) becomes small and slightly negative. This difference occurs because trend components artificially inflate autocorrelation in the original series, and once removed, the residual behaves more like weakly correlated noise.*

c.  Investigate the relationship between the following features and `Type`. Pick two features whose scatter plot gives a good separation between all 6 chart types.

    i.  `linearity`

    ii. `trend_strength`

    iii. `acf1`

    iv. `stl_e_acf1`

    v.  `shift_level_max`

    vi. `shift_var_max`

    vii. `n_crossing_points`

    Make the scatter plot and explain why these features are able to separate the different chart types.

    ```{r}
    train_feats |>
      ggplot(aes(x = linearity, y = shift_var_max, colour = Type)) +
      geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
      labs(
        title = "Linearity vs Maximum Variance Shift",
        x = "Linearity",
        y = "Maximum Variance Shift"
      )
    ```

    *The combination of linearity and shift_var_max provides clear separation between the six control chart types. The feature linearity distinguishes monotonic trends: Increasing types exhibit strong positive linearity while Decreasing types exhibit strong negative linearity.*

    *The feature shift_var_max captures structural breaks and variance changes, separating Upward and Downward types from purely trending series. Cyclic charts exhibit low linearity but high variance shifts due to oscillatory behaviour, while Normal charts exhibit both low linearity and low variance shifts. Together, these two features clearly partition the six chart types in the feature space.*

d.  Install the `caret` package and use the following snippet to fit a $k$-nearest neighbors model on the two features you have selected (substitute `X` and `Y` for the two features you selected in b), and then predict on the test set. What percentage of the test examples are correctly classified? Write code to compute this value. (You should get \> 90% accuracy)

```{r}
#| eval: TRUE
# Change to eval: TRUE in order to run
library(caret)
knn_fit <- train(Type ~ linearity + shift_var_max, data = train_feats, method = "knn")
test_pred <- predict(knn_fit, newdata = test_feats)
accuracy <- mean(test_pred == test_feats$Type) * 100
print(accuracy)
confusionMatrix(test_pred, test_feats$Type)
```

## Question 4 (Periodograms)

Consider the `vic_elec` dataset from the `tsibbledata` package.

```{r}
vic_elec
```

a.  Compute and plot the periodogram for `Demand`. What frequencies and periods do the 7 highest peaks correspond to? *Hint: You may use the* `periodogram` *function provided in* `plot_util.R` *and change the* `max_freq` *setting to see at different resolutions.*

    ```{r}
    source("plot_util.R")
    demand <- vic_elec[['Demand']]
    periodogram(demand, max_freq = 0.5)
    periodogram(demand, max_freq = 0.1)
    periodogram(demand, max_freq = 0.05)
    periodogram(demand, max_freq = 0.01)
    ```

    ```{r}
    n <- length(demand)
    freq <- 0:(n - 1) / n
    per  <- Mod(fft(demand - mean(demand, na.rm = TRUE)))^2

    period_df <- tibble(freq = freq, per = per) |>
      filter(freq > 0, freq <= 0.5) |>
    arrange(desc(per)) |>
    head(7) |>
    mutate(
    period_steps = 1/freq,
    period_hours = period_steps * 0.5,
    period_days = period_hours / 24,
    period_weeks = period_days / 7
    )
    print(period_df)
    ```

b.  Perform an STL decomposition for `Demand`. Plot the periodogram for `season_year`, `season_week`, and `season_day`. Which of the original periodogram peaks appear in each of these periodograms?

    ```{r}
    fit <- vic_elec |>
      model(STL(Demand ~ season(period = "year") + season(period = "week") + season(period = "day"), robust = TRUE))

    cmp <- components(fit)

    periodogram(cmp$season_day, max_freq = 0.10) +
      ggtitle("Periodogram of Daily Seasonal Component") +
      theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))

    periodogram(cmp$season_week, max_freq = 0.02) +
      ggtitle("Periodogram of Weekly Seasonal Component") +
      theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))

    periodogram(cmp$season_year, max_freq = 0.005) +
      ggtitle("Periodogram of Yearly Seasonal Component") +
      theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
    ```

    *After STL decomposition, the periodogram of season_day contains the dominant daily peak (24 hours) and its harmonics (e.g., 12 hours). The periodogram of season_week contains the weekly peak (7 days) and related harmonics (e.g., \~3.5 days). The periodogram of season_year contains the long-period peaks associated with annual and semi-annual seasonality (\~365 and \~182.7 days).* Thus, the STL decomposition separates the original spectral peaks according to their seasonal period.

c.  Based on the peak heights, which is the strongest seasonal component? Does it agree with what you see in a time series decomposition plot?

    ```{r}
    cmp |> autoplot()
    ```

    *The daily seasonal component is strongest because the periodogram has its highest peaks at the daily frequency (24 hours) and its harmonics (e.g., 12 hours). This agrees with the STL decomposition, where the daily seasonal component shows the most prominent repeating pattern relative to the weekly and yearly components.*
